response_parsed.topic: How to manage and dealing with fake content on social media.
response_parsed.opinion:
I've always been curious about how things work, especially when it comes to technology like social media. I'm not sure what you mean by 'dealing'—it sounds like a chore, but honestly, managing fake content should be fun for someone who loves learning and sharing ideas through photography or writing novels.

Dealing with misinformation feels unfair because sometimes people post things that aren't true just to cause trouble or look smart. But I don't let it bother me too much; my neuroticism score is low so stress doesn't really get under my skin when something annoying shows up on my feed. Kansas City isn't a big city, but we still have social media for sure—my husband and son use it more than I do since they're kids.

I think the best way to handle this fake stuff might start with being aware of what's out there without making myself too agreeable or overly conscientious about every little thing people post. Maybe checking sources before clicking share, like maybe using that photography skill to capture good evidence instead of just believing whatever pops up first.

I'm also a Muslim woman living here—I don't think my religion adds much advice here except staying grounded with low neuroticism helps me not get worked up over fake controversies online.
response_parsed.reasoning_summary: Fake content on social media can be frustrating due to misinformation, but I prefer focusing on the positive aspects like sharing accurate ideas through photography or novels. My low conscientiousness and agreeableness make me less concerned with being right all the time; instead, I rely on my curiosity to investigate things calmly because of moderate neuroticism.
